title: SDL ServiceFlow
description: A ServiceFlow is a named cross-service business flow — a sequence of interactions across multiple service boundaries to accomplish a business outcome. ServiceFlows live at layer_service: they describe how services talk to each other from both a business perspective (what use case is being fulfilled) and optionally a sequence perspective (the technical call order). Each step in a ServiceFlow is a DL inter-Container Connect owned by the platform Container.

perspectives.name: business
perspectives.description: What business outcome this flow achieves. Written in plain language for domain experts and architects: who initiates it, what the outcome is, what can go wrong. Always required.

perspectives.name: sequence
perspectives.description: The technical call order between services — protocols, sync vs async, topics. Written for engineers auditing cross-service coupling and integration patterns. Optional.

dl.notes: ServiceFlow = Flow (ordered cross-service steps) + Connect (each step is an inter-Container Connect at layer_platform, owned by the platform Container). Steps reference service ids from the PlatformManifest and optionally entry_point_ref / dependency_ref to link back to layer_logic primitives.

id.description: Unique kebab-case identifier for this flow.

label.description: Human-readable flow name. Write as a noun phrase describing the business outcome.

description.description: One paragraph describing what triggers this flow, what it accomplishes, and who participates.

trigger.description: What initiates this flow — the external actor or system event that starts the cross-service choreography.

actor.description: Who or what initiates the flow. References an actor kind from stdlib/service-flow-actor-kinds.json or a free-form name.

description.description: What the actor does to start the flow, in plain language.
examples: Customer submits their cart via POST /orders
Payment gateway webhook fires for payment.completed

entry_point_ref.description: Optional link to the specific EntryPoint in a service's entry-points.sdl.json that receives this trigger.

business.description: The intent perspective on this flow. Describes the business use case in plain language — no technical call order, no protocols. Audience: domain experts, architects, engineers auditing AI-designed cross-service interactions.

summary.description: One or two sentences: what business goal does this cross-service flow accomplish?
examples: A customer submits their cart and, upon successful payment, receives a confirmed order with fulfilment initiated.
An admin or the customer cancels an order, triggering stock release and a refund if payment was made.

actors.description: All participants in this flow — external actors and services. Written as display names.

outcome.description: The observable business state after this flow completes successfully.
examples: Order is confirmed, fulfilment is initiated, customer is notified.
Order is cancelled, stock is released, refund is issued.

failure_modes.description: Meaningful failure cases from a business perspective — what goes wrong across service boundaries, and how the platform responds.

examples: Stock unavailable when order is placed
Order rejected at order-service. Customer shown unavailable items. No payment intent created.

steps.description: The ordered sequence of cross-service interactions. Each step is an inter-Container Connect between services. Step ids: '1', '2', etc. for sequential; '2.a', '2.b' for parallel. Mirrors the id convention in Operation routing.steps[].

id.description: Step identifier. Sequential: '1', '2'. Parallel at same level: '2.a', '2.b'.

from_service.description: The service (or external actor) initiating this interaction. Use service ids from the PlatformManifest, or actor names for external actors.

to_service.description: The service receiving this interaction. Must reference a service id in the PlatformManifest.

label.description: What this interaction does, in plain language.

protocol.description: The communication protocol for this interaction.

style.description: Synchronous (caller waits for response) or asynchronous (event-driven, fire-and-forget).

topic.description: For async interactions: the event topic or queue name.

parallel.description: When true, this step runs concurrently with other steps at the same id level (e.g., '2.a' and '2.b' are parallel).

entry_point_ref.description: Link to the specific EntryPoint in the target service that receives this interaction.

dependency_ref.description: Link to the specific Dependency in the source service that makes this call.

shape_ref.description: The data shape passed in this interaction.

service_id.description: The service that owns the DataShape.

data_shape_id.description: The data shape id in that service's data-shapes.sdl.json.

notes.description: Design rationale or non-obvious integration notes for this step.

sequence.description: The implementation perspective on this flow. Describes the technical call order, protocols, and integration patterns in detail. Optional — the business perspective is the primary artifact.

notes.description: Technical notes on the call sequence — timing, ordering constraints, retry behaviour, idempotency across services.

diagram_ref.description: Path or URL to a sequence diagram for this flow.
